models.name: Theory of Constraints
models.one_line: Throughput is set by the single binding constraint; optimise there, ignore the rest.

models.name: Second-Order Thinking
models.one_line: Ask 'and then what?' — the third hop is where mispricing lives.

models.name: Inversion
models.one_line: Solve the failure case first; avoid what kills the goal rather than chasing it.

models.name: Reflexivity
models.one_line: Observation changes the system — a map accurate enough to trade on gets arbitraged away.

models.name: Red Queen / Creative Destruction
models.one_line: Every rent decays; every choke breeds bypass; date-stamp all concentration bets.

models.name: Margin of Safety
models.one_line: Build buffer against error; the dangerous quadrant is high conviction + thin margin.

models.name: Circle of Competence
models.one_line: Know the edge boundary; delegate out-of-circle rather than trusting your own judgment.

models.name: Survivorship Bias
models.one_line: Study failures as carefully as successes — the graveyard holds the most useful data.

models.name: Regression to the Mean
models.one_line: Extreme outcomes revert; the base rate always reasserts.

models.name: Goodhart / Gresham
models.one_line: When a measure becomes a target, it ceases to be a good measure.

models.name: Critical Mass / Slowly-then-all-at-once
models.one_line: Forming windows have a flip threshold; name the exact event that crosses it.

models.name: Scale Laws
models.one_line: What works at 1× breaks at 10×; ask the question before you need the answer.

models.name: 12 Leverage Points
models.one_line: Places to intervene in a system, ranked weakest to strongest — most people push on the weakest ones (parameters) because they're the easiest to see.
models.source: Donella Meadows, "Leverage Points: Places to Intervene in a System" (1999)

ranked_list.point: Buffer sizes relative to the flows they must absorb
Structure of material stocks and flows (transport networks, population age structures)
Length of delays relative to the rate of system change
Strength of negative (balancing) feedback loops relative to what they're correcting
Gain around driving positive (reinforcing) feedback loops
Structure of information flows — who does and doesn't have access to what
Rules of the system — incentives, punishments, constraints
Power to add, change, evolve, or self-organize system structure
Mindset or paradigm the system arises out of

models.name: Value Created vs Value Captured
models.one_line: Creating value and keeping value are different games — a layer can do enormous good and still capture almost none of it.
models.source: general value-chain strategy, in the lineage of Porter's value chain

models.name: 7 Powers
models.one_line: Durable differential returns come from exactly seven structural barriers — scale/network economies, counter-positioning, switching costs, branding, cornered resource, process power — everything else decays to competitive parity.

models.name: Value Migration
models.one_line: Profit pools relocate between industries and layers over time, usually away from wherever the crowd is still counting on it staying put.

models.name: Smiling Curve
models.one_line: Plot margin against position in a value chain and it smiles — highest at the two ends (R&D/IP and brand/service), lowest in the assembly middle.

models.name: Picks and Shovels
models.one_line: In a gold rush, sell the tool everyone needs regardless of who strikes gold — capture sits upstream of who wins the contest.
models.source: Gold Rush aphorism, standard in venture/investing strategy

models.name: Aggregation Theory
models.one_line: Whoever owns the user or demand relationship captures the value, because they can commoditize their suppliers at will — the aggregator's switching-cost moat is the tell.
